worth_it_explanation: DoubleVerify's Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) offers a configurable set of suitability categories, each with up to three severity tiers. Accounts that accept the vendor defaults without reviewing them against a documented brand policy are making a spending decision by omission. The categories and tier thresholds determine which inventory the account avoids, and defaults are calibrated for a median advertiser, not for any specific brand. These checks require no external tooling: reviewing and adjusting category and tier settings takes place directly in DV Pinnacle. The time investment is small relative to the risk of funding inventory the brand has explicitly marked as off-limits.
intro: DoubleVerify's ABS lets advertisers configure which content categories to monitor or block, and for categories that carry tiered risk, how severe the content must be before it triggers that action. The system classifies inventory across more than 100 content categories, with a subset of categories offering three severity tiers: low risk (educational or informational treatment), medium risk (news, dramatic, or entertainment treatment), and high risk (user-generated or irresponsible treatment). These checks confirm that the enabled categories and tier thresholds reflect a deliberate, brand-specific policy rather than a vendor default, and that the same profile does not blanket campaigns whose audiences and contexts warrant different settings.

audit_checks.title: ABS suitability categories reflect vendor defaults rather than brand policy
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A suitability profile set to vendor defaults rather than brand policy means the account is either more permissive than the brand intends, accepting adjacency to content the brand has explicitly marked as off-limits, or more restrictive than necessary, suppressing valid inventory and reducing reach without a documented reason. Because DV Pinnacle applies the profile pre-bid or post-bid across all inventory the account touches, the effect of misconfigured defaults compounds at scale.

educate.base:
DoubleVerify's Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) system classifies inventory across more than 100 content categories. For each category, an advertiser can choose to block impressions, monitor them without blocking, or take no action. When a brand activates DV without reviewing the default category settings, the account inherits DoubleVerify's baseline configuration, which is calibrated for a broad median advertiser rather than for the specific brand's risk tolerance.

The IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy underpins much of the content classification that DV and similar vendors use to categorize inventory. Within DV's implementation, the mapping between raw content signals and actionable categories determines which impressions the account avoids. A brand whose documented safety policy differs from the vendor's defaults may be funding adjacency it has explicitly prohibited, or forgoing reach it would have approved.

A few signs point to the profile being set at or near vendor defaults:
  • The suitability profile in DV Pinnacle was created at account setup and has not been reviewed since
  • Categories that the brand's documented policy marks as off-limits appear in a 'monitor' state rather than 'block'
  • Categories that the brand has no policy concern about appear blocked, suppressing inventory without a brand rationale
  • No record exists of the profile being compared against a written brand safety brief
You may want to request the brand's internal content policy or brand safety brief before making changes to the profile, so the adjustments reflect documented decisions rather than assumptions.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, categories related to tragedy and conflict, sensitive social issues, and certain user-generated content contexts are worth reviewing against the brand's public-facing identity and the audiences it reaches. A family-oriented retailer may want stricter blocking than a default profile provides, while a retailer targeting adults may have flexibility on categories the default treats as blocked.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, content adjacency affects form completion intent as much as it affects brand reputation. Categories tied to anxiety-inducing or sensationalist content can suppress conversion rates even when the ad is served correctly. You may want to confirm that the enabled blocks align with the emotional context the brand wants to associate with its offer.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify's documentation on ABS category configuration and the full list of suitability categories is available in DV Pinnacle and through DV's partner knowledge base, which requires a DV account login to access. Contact your DoubleVerify account team for the category list and configuration guide.

impactful_updates.update_text: The industry's previous shared reference framework for brand safety and suitability categories, GARM (Global Alliance for Responsible Media), was discontinued in August 2024 when the World Federation of Advertisers disbanded the initiative. DoubleVerify's own ABS category taxonomy continues to operate independently of GARM, and the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy remains the active public standard underpinning content classification across the industry.

investigate.intro: Open DV Pinnacle and review the active suitability profile against the brand's documented content policy. Note which categories are blocked, which are monitored, and which are inactive.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the Brand Safety section for the advertiser account.
Locate the active Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profile. Note the profile name and when it was last modified.

Review the full list of suitability categories. For each category, record whether it is set to 'Block', 'Monitor', or inactive (no action). Focus on categories the brand's policy addresses explicitly.

Compare the current settings against the brand's written content policy or brand safety brief. Note any category where the DV setting is more permissive than the policy requires, or more restrictive than the policy allows.
Check whether more than one suitability profile exists in the account. If so, confirm which campaigns or DSP line items each profile applies to.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner compares the active ABS profile against the brand's documented content policy and records discrepancies. An account whose category settings map directly to a reviewed and documented brand policy, with block or monitor states that match the stated risk tolerance for each category, passes. An account whose profile has not been reviewed against any documented policy, or where categories the policy marks as off-limits are set to monitor-only or inactive, is a fail recorded with the specific categories and their current states.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the current state of each relevant suitability category above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DoubleVerify Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profile appears to reflect vendor defaults rather than a reviewed brand policy. Categories the brand's documented guidelines mark as off-limits are set to monitor-only or inactive, meaning impressions in those contexts are recorded but not blocked. {{voice_recommend}} requesting a full export of the current ABS category settings from DV Pinnacle, comparing each setting against the brand's written content policy, and updating the profile so block or monitor states align with documented risk tolerance for each category. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a review cadence for the profile. New content categories and tier adjustments from DoubleVerify require an active decision about whether the default setting fits the brand.

audit_checks.title: Severity tiers for tiered categories are not calibrated to brand risk tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: DoubleVerify assigns three severity tiers to a subset of its suitability categories: low risk (educational or informational treatment), medium risk (news, dramatic, or entertainment treatment), and high risk (user-generated or irresponsible treatment). An account that blocks or monitors all three tiers uniformly, regardless of the brand's actual concern about each treatment level, either over-suppresses inventory by blocking low-risk educational content alongside high-risk user-generated content, or under-protects the brand by applying the same mild monitoring action to content the brand would want blocked entirely.

educate.base:
For suitability categories that carry severity tiers, DoubleVerify distinguishes three levels of risk based on how a topic is treated in the content rather than whether the topic appears at all. Low risk covers educational or informational contexts, such as a scientific article discussing a sensitive medical condition. Medium risk covers news reporting, dramatic entertainment, or journalistic coverage of the same topic. High risk covers user-generated, unmoderated, or irresponsible treatment of the topic.

This tiered structure means a brand can engage with nuance: it might accept low-risk educational content involving a sensitive topic while blocking high-risk user-generated content on the same subject. Applying a single blocking or monitoring action across all three tiers removes that nuance. A brand that blocks all three tiers of a category involving, for example, health and medical topics may inadvertently exclude reach on reputable health publications. A brand that monitors all three tiers uniformly may fail to block the high-risk user-generated content it actually wants to avoid.

A few signs point to the severity tiers being uncalibrated:
  • Tiered categories in the ABS profile are set to a single action (all-block or all-monitor) with no differentiation by tier
  • The account team cannot explain why a specific tier threshold was chosen for any given category
  • The tier settings have not changed since the account was first configured
You may want to review the brand's position on each tiered category before adjusting the thresholds. What constitutes acceptable treatment of a sensitive topic is a brand-specific decision.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, tiered categories related to alcohol, gambling, or sensitive social issues may warrant different treatment depending on the brand's product mix and audience. A broad-market retailer may accept low-risk educational content while blocking high-risk user-generated treatment of those topics. You may want to confirm the current tier thresholds reflect that intent rather than a default uniform action.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, the context in which a tiered category appears can affect both brand perception and conversion intent. News or journalistic treatment of a sensitive topic (medium risk) may be acceptable for some brands while user-generated treatment (high risk) is not. You may want to confirm the tier thresholds are set to block the contexts the brand genuinely wants to avoid rather than all contexts indiscriminately.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify's documentation on which categories carry severity tiers and the specific definition of each tier is available in DV Pinnacle and through DV's partner knowledge base, which requires a DV account login. Contact your DoubleVerify account team for the tiered category list and configuration options.

investigate.intro: In DV Pinnacle, identify the categories that carry severity tiers and review what tier threshold is configured for each. Confirm whether the threshold reflects a deliberate brand decision.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and open the active Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profile.

Filter or sort the category list to show only the categories that offer tiered severity settings. These are the categories where DV allows you to configure whether low, medium, or high risk triggers the block or monitor action.
For each tiered category that is active in the profile, note the tier threshold selected and the action applied (block or monitor).
Ask the account team or brand contact whether the tier threshold for each active tiered category reflects a documented decision. Record any category where the threshold cannot be explained.

Note whether any tiered categories are set to block all three tiers uniformly. For those, assess whether the brand has a documented reason to block educational or informational content (low risk) alongside user-generated content (high risk) on the same topic.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the tier threshold for each active tiered category and confirms whether it reflects a documented brand decision. A tiered category with a threshold that matches the brand's documented policy for that topic area passes. A tiered category where the threshold is set uniformly across all tiers without a documented rationale, or where the threshold is inconsistent with the brand's stated risk tolerance, is a fail recorded with the category name, the current threshold, and the expected threshold based on the brand's policy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the tier thresholds for each active tiered category above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DoubleVerify Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profile applies uniform tier thresholds across tiered categories, without calibrating the block or monitor action to the specific treatment level that triggers the brand's concern. For categories where DoubleVerify distinguishes between educational, journalistic, and user-generated content, the current configuration either over-suppresses inventory by treating all three tiers identically or under-protects the brand by applying the same mild action to high-risk user-generated content and low-risk educational content alike. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each tiered category against the brand's documented content policy and setting the tier threshold at the level that matches the brand's actual concern for that topic. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the rationale for each threshold so future reviewers can assess whether the setting still reflects brand policy.

audit_checks.title: A single suitability profile is applied across campaigns that warrant different settings
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When one ABS profile governs all campaigns regardless of audience, creative context, or campaign goal, the account makes a forced trade-off on every impression. A profile calibrated for the most sensitive campaign in the account over-restricts inventory for campaigns where a broader suitability posture is appropriate. A profile calibrated for the least sensitive campaign leaves the most brand-sensitive placements under-protected. Either outcome represents a misconfiguration, and the effect accumulates across every impression the account delivers.

educate.base:
DoubleVerify supports multiple Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profiles within a single advertiser account. Each profile can carry a different combination of category settings and tier thresholds, and profiles can be associated with specific demand-side platform (DSP) line items or campaigns rather than applied account-wide.

The need for more than one profile arises when campaigns differ meaningfully in audience, creative content, or business context. A campaign running awareness video on connected television (CTV) and a campaign running direct-response display to an adult audience may have legitimately different brand safety requirements. Applying the more restrictive profile to both limits reach on the direct-response campaign unnecessarily. Applying the more permissive profile to both exposes the awareness campaign to content contexts the brand would not approve for that placement type.

A few signs point to a single profile being applied where multiple are warranted:
  • The DV Pinnacle account shows only one ABS profile, and that profile is associated with all DSP line items in the account
  • Campaigns targeting audiences with different age ranges or content sensitivities share the same suitability settings
  • Line items running on CTV, online video, and display carry identical suitability configurations despite the different content contexts of those environments
You may want to confirm with the brand or media team whether the current campaigns warrant differentiated profiles before building additional configurations. The added management overhead of multiple profiles is only justified when the campaigns genuinely differ in their risk posture.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running simultaneous campaigns for different product lines, the brand safety context may differ between lines. A campaign for children's products and a campaign for home improvement tools may warrant different suitability profiles even within the same account. You may want to confirm whether the current profile reflects the most restrictive product line or a compromise between them.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser with campaigns across different verticals or funnel stages, top-of-funnel awareness campaigns and bottom-of-funnel retargeting campaigns may call for different suitability settings. Awareness placements running on premium inventory may accept a different content posture than retargeting placements on open exchange inventory.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify's documentation on creating and managing multiple Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profiles and associating them with specific DSP line items is available in DV Pinnacle and through DV's partner knowledge base, both of which require a DV account login. Contact your DoubleVerify account team for guidance on multi-profile setup.

investigate.intro: In DV Pinnacle, confirm how many ABS profiles exist in the account and which campaigns or DSP line items each profile governs. Then assess whether the campaigns the account is running warrant more than one profile.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the Brand Safety section. List all Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profiles available in the account.
For each profile, note which DSP line items or campaigns it is associated with. If a single profile is associated with all line items, record that.
Review the active campaigns in the account. Note whether they differ by audience age range, placement type (connected television, online video, display), creative content, or campaign objective.

For campaigns that differ on any of the above dimensions, assess whether the current single profile applies the same category settings and tier thresholds to all of them. Record any campaign pairings where the same profile is applied but the campaigns have meaningfully different content safety requirements.
Note whether any existing profile has a documented rationale that explains which campaign types it is designed for.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists the ABS profiles in the account, maps each profile to the campaigns it governs, and assesses whether campaigns with materially different audience or content contexts share a profile. An account with a single profile applied uniformly passes only when all active campaigns genuinely share the same brand safety requirements and that equivalence is documented. An account where campaigns with different audience compositions, placement types, or content contexts share a single profile without documented justification is a fail recorded with the campaign pairings and the dimension on which they differ.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the profiles present in the account and the campaigns each profile governs above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DoubleVerify account applies a single Authentic Brand Suitability (ABS) profile across campaigns that differ in audience, placement type, or content context. A uniform profile forces a trade-off on every impression: settings calibrated for the most sensitive campaign over-restrict inventory for other campaigns, while settings calibrated for the least sensitive campaign leave higher-risk placements under-protected. {{voice_recommend}} inventorying the active campaigns and identifying where audience composition, placement environment, or creative content creates a genuine difference in brand safety requirements. For campaign groupings where that difference is material, {{voice_recommend}} creating a dedicated ABS profile calibrated to each grouping's risk posture and associating it with the relevant DSP line items in DV Pinnacle.
